element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player learns paddle control and basic tile mechanics in early waves
Core mechanic - forming unbroken horizontal lines of 3+ same-colored tiles
Adds complexity to spatial awareness within the well columns
Requires advanced pattern recognition and well planning
Bonus points for Klaxs formed by falling tiles after prior matches
Player can choose progression difficulty and earn bonus points for later wave selection
Core resource management - tiles missed result in 'drops'; well fills from bottom up
Advanced scoring mechanic requiring precise tile placement
Each wave has unique win condition (Klax count, points, tile survival threshold)
Player can pull down joystick to speed up tile descent; speed increases with wave difficulty

typical_relief_triggers: Bonus tile catch before well full
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Rhythmic tension-release cycle driven by tile flow rate and well fill state. Early waves allow deliberate placement with relief pauses; mid-to-late waves accelerate tile descent and objective difficulty, forcing reactive play with tighter windows between Klax completions and well overflow. Wave completion provides distinct relief beats every 5 waves with player agency in difficulty selection.

difficulty_curve.early_game: Introductory waves (1-20) teach basic tile catching and single-line Klax formation. Drop meter resets frequently; objective requirements are modest (low Klax count or point thresholds). Player learns paddle control and well column mechanics with low penalty pressure.
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Waves 21-60 introduce diagonal matching, cascading chains, and increasingly aggressive tile speeds. Objectives diversify (specific Klax types, tile survival counts). Drop penalties accumulate faster. Well management becomes critical as placement errors compound. Wave selection decisions impact progression path and bonus point opportunities.
difficulty_curve.late_game: Waves 61-100 feature rapid tile descent, complex objective combinations (simultaneous point and Klax targets), and severe well-fill consequences. Cascading mechanics become essential for score and objective completion. Drop meter becomes limiting resource; player must maintain near-perfect tile catch and placement efficiency. Final waves demand mastery of all mechanics and spatial prediction.
notable_spikes: First introduction of diagonal Klax patterns (mid-early waves)
Wave speed acceleration threshold (approximately wave 30-40 region)
Objective complexity increase when tile survival becomes primary condition (mid-late transition)
Final wave group (95-100) combines all mechanics at maximum speed with compound objectives

notable_level_patterns.description: 100 waves organized into 20 groups of 5, with wave selection decision points. Each wave presents distinct objective types: (1) Klax count targets, (2) point thresholds, (3) tile survival minimums. Objectives increase in stringency per group.

Drop penalties accumulate throughout 5-wave groups but reset to zero after each group completion. Wave selection decision offers player agency to pursue later (harder) waves for bonus points or earlier (safer) waves to maintain penalty buffer.

Tile descent speed increases within each 5-wave group and again between groups, creating micro and macro difficulty curves. Player can push down to accelerate further for tighter control but increased input demand.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Wave 1 baseline speed → wave 5 elevated → wave 6 reset but higher baseline → escalation continues

notable_level_patterns.description: Well holds 25 tiles (5 columns × 5 rows). Geometry of Klax formations (horizontal, vertical, diagonal) creates puzzle sub-patterns requiring spatial planning. Filled well ends the run; empty spaces provide bonus points at wave end.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Any wave requiring specific pattern formation before well fills
